Jung on Film: Post-Jungian Takes on the Moving Image edited by Christopher Hauke Alister (Brunner-Routledge, Hove and Philadelphia, 2001). 254 pp. £16.99.

Emilija Kiehl

Emilija Kiehl

Bow Road, London E3 4LL

Emilija Kiehl  

Film is undoubtedly the most popular art form of our times. It emerged at the end of the nineteenth century, at the same time as depth psychology, and together they marked the beginning of ‘modernity’. Film was born of modern consciousness. It develops and changes in correspondence with the changes of the collective psyche. The experience of watching a film can be compared to the experience in a Jungian psychotherapy session: it is a special place where the psyche can come alive, be experienced and be commented upon. The director who produces a film is not unlike the Self that creates (produces) dreams, but is the psychological analysis of a film an act of imposition on what the director was trying to express? Alternatively, does the power of the film itself evoke in the analyst a response which their experience and understanding move them to articulate in psychological terms?

These are the fundamental ideas of the book Jung on Film, a collection of essays by Jungian therapists from both sides of the Atlantic, on 116 films. It is divided into three parts: ‘A Jungian Perspective’, ‘Four Films and a Director’ and ‘Studies in Genres and Gender’.

An excellent Introduction by the editors clearly presents the Jungian perspective on psychic life and on film analysis which includes the themes of psychological transformation and the ‘alchemy of our conscious-unconscious processes’. The glossary at the end of the book explains Jungian terminology.

In Part I, ‘A Jungian Perspective’, the essay by Don Frederickson, ‘Jung/sign/symbol/film’ draws a distinction between the Freudian (semiotic) and the Jungian (symbolic) perspective, and the difference between what Jung saw as symbol, as opposed to sign:

The symbol is alive only so long as it is pregnant with meaning. But once the meaning has been born out of it, once the expression is found that formulates the thing expected, or divined even better than the hitherto accepted symbol, the symbol is dead, i.e. it possesses only an historical significance.

Christina Wieland  

This is a beautifully written book with a good balance of theoretical and clinical sections. The clinical material is detailed and thought provoking and gives the reader a very good insight into the way in which Mitrani works. The author stresses in the Introduction that the book ‘is not intended as a guide to analytic practice, it is solely intended as an example of how one analyst works’. This she does extremely well. The book is very moving and I enjoyed reading it. However, it raised more questions for me than it answered. In this sense it is a book to be read and debated.

The book is underpinned by the theories of Tustin, Bick, Meltzer, and others who deal with autistic, and early pre-symbolic states, with the concept of adhesive identification as the central defence of these states.

Andrzej Werbart

Andrzej Werbart  

As humans we cope with incomprehensible and threatening experiences by creating our own theories about what is happening. In this study two psychotic patients and their therapists were asked to describe the patient's problem, its background and preferential treatment. To get a third perspective, Rorschach was administered to the patients. The interviews were repeated 6 and 18 months later. The narratives were compared and related to the outcome patterns. The most apparent difference was found in the theories about the cure. The Rorschach assessment was found to complement the interviews in a fruitful way.

Thirty Years of Abdominal Pain

Julian Stern MRCPsych

Julian Stern

Watford Road, Harrow
Middlesex HA1 3UJ
e-mail: julianstern@hotmail.com

Julian Stern  

In this paper, the author describes a brief psychotherapy with a man who has struggled with abdominal symptoms for most of his adult life. After an unhappy childhood, the patient (Mr A) married and then was witness to the birth of his stillborn child, in a foreign country. Soon after his abdominal symptoms started, and plagued him for the following 30 years. In the therapy, Mr A began to explore areas of guilt, hostility, and shame, together with previously unknown-about unconscious phantasies. By the end of the therapy, he reported a substantial diminution in his symptoms, as well as changes to his relationships, and mode of thinking and feeling. Some aspects of working psychoanalytically with patients with somatic symptoms, especially those pertaining to the gastrointestinal tract, are discussed.

Rosie Davis

Rosie Davis

Bow Road, London E3 4LL

Rosie Davis  

As an SHO in psychiatry, and having started a psychodynamic long case in the past few months, finding an introductory text that imparted useful information in an easily digestible form was difficult. Now recognized as a key requirement for training towards membership of the Royal College of Psychiatrists, gaining experience in one of the psychotherapies (and having an understanding of the others) is taking on a renewed significance for junior psychiatry trainees.

Sidney Bloch's short text was a welcome primer for the psychotherapy novice. Accessible and exceptionally readable, the authors struck successfully a fine balance between giving enough background to orientate the newcomer to a clear level of understanding, and including too much detail at the expense of readability. That said, each chapter encapsulated sufficient detail to allow reference to important writings and studies in the literature, associated with each particular therapy (see, in particular, the chapters on behavioural and cognitive approaches). Where some of the evidence cited was more than 20 years old, those studies tended to be by some of the most influential researchers in the field, e.g. Masters and Johnson in sex therapy or Aaron Beck on cognitive distortions.

From psychodynamic therapies (individual, long-term, short-term, and group), crisis intervention, through to behavioural and cognitive disciplines, onto couple and sex therapies, family and child, and supportive psychotherapies, the text ended on a laudable note by discussing ethical issues. This closing chapter, by T. Byram Karasu, addressed some of the more controversial areas in psychotherapy, for example the ‘repressed memory controversy’ and the research limitations of proving validity or efficacy. He commented that there are unresolved research problems in the field of psychotherapy. He alluded to certain factors, including the anecdotal nature of individual case reports, the elusiveness of many basic concepts (such as transference or defence mechanisms), and the complexity of the patient-therapist relationship, as being difficult to ‘measure’ in a standardized way for the purposes of research.

[[RunningHead]]

Developing the Therapeutic Alliance in Acute Mental Health Care

Ian Thurston

Ian Thurston

Claremont House
Newcastle upon Tyne NE2 4AA
e-mail: Ian@thurston782.freeserve.co.uk

Ian Thurston  

The establishment of a good working relationship, or therapeutic alliance, is seen as a key indicator of good outcome in all forms of psychological therapy. Such a relationship, however, is difficult to establish in acute mental health, when the patient may both need and fear therapeutic contact. The patient care plan can give the appearance of a positive working alliance when this is not truly established. The Care Plan Approach can prioritize case management and risk assessment over treatment. A good therapeutic alliance can often be achieved in times of acute crisis, but only when the difficulties in so doing are recognized and acknowledged. The distinction between benign and malign dependency can be a useful way of conceptualizing therapeutic and non-therapeutic factors in acute care. Apparently progressive paradigms in mental health care serve to deny the inherent difficulties and conflicts involved in establishing a therapeutic alliance in cases of severe mental illness. Developing a genuine therapeutic culture in acute mental health requires that organizational structures and protocols are clinically informed and are congruent with the therapeutic ethos desired. Examples from a recently established Acute Day Hospital will illustrate how a psychodynamically-informed group-based treatment can be effective in developing the therapeutic alliance. I will focus on Psychiatric Nursing, although I hope that the content of the paper is more widely applicable.

John Wright

John Wright

Flat 2, The Square
Ermington

John Wright  

This paper explores how a psychoanalytic perspective can be useful in understanding a particular defensive organization based around racism. A series of clinical vignettes are presented of material from an adolescent patient in psychotherapy. These vignettes concern the functional significance of a blatant racist discourse presented in therapy as a defence against a traumatized and denigrated identity. The clinical concepts of splitting and projective identification enabled a clearer understanding of the patient's presentation and progression of treatment rather than reaching an impasse.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Memorizing Vs Understanding

Abraham Brafman

Abraham Brafman

20 Hollycroft Avenue
London NW3 7QL
ahbrafman@onetel.net.uk

Abraham Brafman  

I have tried to illustrate my experience that medical students can find certain difficulties in dealing with psychiatric patients that are not frequently experienced in other medical disciplines. While other patients are seen to harbour a well-defined cause for their pathology and, particularly, for being hospitalized, psychiatric patients will sometimes be seen as ‘normal people’ going through some personal crisis that, in the students’ experience of life, should not warrant the diagnosis of a medical condition. The educational setting in which these patients are met means that students may listen to what their seniors are teaching and memorize concepts and their definitions without, necessarily, comprehending the rationale for these. In the specific field of psychotherapy, this problem becomes even more difficult to spot, since so many psychoanalytic concepts have become part of everyday language. Projection, unconscious, transference and other such terms are used very often, even though the meanings and implications of each concept may elude the user.

I put particular emphasis on my finding that, fundamentally, learning demands the existence of a previous ‘not-knowing,’ where the new datum will fill a gap or explain a quandary. When this is not present, an intelligent student is well able to memorize what he is supposed to absorb, but this will only be translated into actual learning when that awareness of a gap is experienced by the student. This is why I believe that simply giving definitions of concepts does not equip the student to grasp where to fit in the new datum.

Another point made in the paper is a discussion of the relevance of trying to help the students to become aware of the feelings that patients bring out in them. This is especially important in psychiatry, where patients can sometimes produce a strong emotional impact on the student. Most students see such feelings as a sign that they are becoming personally involved or being judgmental and, therefore, they try hard to dismiss or deny these impressions. These feelings will influence the student's approach to the patient and, in a more subtle way, his capacity to assess the patient, hence it is important to discuss how any emotional reaction to the encounter with the patient must be acknowledged and scrutinized. I have argued that to categorize, ab initio, these feelings as ‘countertransference’ is not only wrong, but it deprives the student of an opportunity to learn about himself and his approach to patients.

Internal and External Reality in In-Patient Psychotherapy: Working with Severely Disturbed Patients at the Cassel Hospital11

Wilhelm Skogstad

Wilhelm Skogstad

1 Ham Common
Richmond, Surrey
TW10 7JF.

Wilhelm Skogstad  

The author argues, on the basis of Steiner's theory of ‘psychic retreats’, that inpatient treatment is always at risk of turning into an unhelpful refuge. Thoughts on this problem and on the use of external reality in inpatient treatment are traced in the literature. He suggests that the combination of working on aspects of internal reality in psychotherapy and working on the corresponding aspects of external reality in psychosocial nursing can have a powerful therapeutic effect and can help to avoid treatment turning into a refuge. The setting of the Cassel Hospital is described and, using various case material, the author shows how a patient's internal dynamic can present itself in these two different arenas and can be worked on simultaneously in quite different ways. Crucial in this concept is the co-operation of staff with the aim of understanding the dynamics between staff and patients and between members of staff. The paper concludes by looking at the mutual influences within the institutional dynamics of the hospital.

A Description of An Audit Cycle of Early Dropouts from An Inpatient Psychotherapy Unit

Marco Chiesa MD MRCPsych , Miriam Wright BSc and Rebecca Neeld RMN

Rebecca Neeld

TW10 7JF, UK
e-mail: m.chiesa@ucl.ac.uk

Rebecca Neeld  

This paper describes the results and implications of an 8-year audit cycle concerning premature termination of treatment in a population with severe personality disorder treated within a specialist residential setting. The results of the first audit showed (a) a high early dropout rate, (b) a significant difference in early dropout rates between the two clinical programmes (one-stage and step-down) offered by the unit, and (c) a higher attrition of non-borderline personality disorders compared to borderline type disorder. Qualitative analysis of interviews with early dropout patients helped to identify deficiencies in practice, which may have contributed to dropout. Discussion of results amongst the team led to corrective measures that allowed reduction of early dropout in the one-stage programme over the subsequent years. The implications of the results are discussed.

responseSet.title: Mental Illness, the Psychotic Wavelength and the Mental Health Act (1983)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mental Illness, the Psychotic Wavelength and the Mental Health Act (1983)

George Ikkos and Christine Wee

Christine Wee

Brockley Hill
Stanmore, Middlesex HA7 4LP

St Mary's Rotation, London

Christine Wee  

The authors explore the contribution of the psychodynamic approach to the assessment of suspected psychotic disorder under the provisions of The Mental Health Act (1983). They advocate that attention to descriptive psychopathology needs to be complemented by attention to psychodynamic psychopathology. Psychodynamic psychopathology has a contribution to make both to the direct assessment of the patient and to the management of the countertransference. Successful management of the countertransference will facilitate the empathic assessment of the patient and the secure containment of psychopathology. The limitations of the psychodynamic approach to the psychoses are acknowledged.

responseSet.title: On the Therapeutic Value of Not Offering Psychotherapy: An Account of An Extended Assessment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Therapeutic Value of Not Offering Psychotherapy: An Account of An Extended Assessment

Laurence Spurling

Laurence Spurling

Malet Street, London WC1 7HX
e-mail: l.spurling@bbk.ac.uk

Laurence Spurling  

This paper looks at why the assessment of patients for treatment in a hospital psychotherapy department should be such a source of strain. It is argued that a major cause of this strain is the need to manage a role conflict between acting as a therapist or gatekeeper. This role conflict is often experienced as a sense of guilt in the assessor about depriving the patient of a valuable commodity, psychotherapy. An example of an extended assessment with a particular patient is given to illustrate how this role conflict might manifest itself, in this case in the struggle by both patient and therapist to find value in the decision not to offer long-term treatment. Some of the transference implications of arriving at and communicating a judgement regarding treatment are then discussed. Finally the question is addressed as to whether the decision made was the right or wrong one, and what it means to think of assessments in these terms.

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA.

Judith Trowell  

This quite slim volume is aimed at psychotherapists in the UK, and its main message is that the legacy of slavery is very important in understanding the problems and treatment needs of Afro-Caribbean patients now living in the UK. It does this by demonstrating how the history over generations of trauma, betrayal, loss, disruptions and humiliations, makes vital the constant awareness in any therapy of the internal-external world boundary, so that not all psychopathology is ascribed to internal-world difficulties.

responseSet.title: Dilemmas in Providing Psychological Therapy as An Integral Component of Mental Health Care
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dilemmas in Providing Psychological Therapy as An Integral Component of Mental Health Care

Anne Goodwin

Anne Goodwin

Mansfield Road, Sutton in Ashfield
Notts NG17 4JT.

Anne Goodwin  

Our notions of what constitutes a good therapeutic relationship come from models developed for people with high levels of personal resources who actively choose therapy in preference to other ways of addressing their problems. The context is quite different for those with more severe and enduring mental health problems, when therapy can be one of many interventions being provided. Some of the dilemmas that arise within the therapeutic relationship in such cases can be understood in terms of the therapeutic relationship being conceived of within the dynamics of a dyad, when the actual situation is more akin to a triadic relationship comprising the client, the therapist and others involved in the client's care. Examples of threats to the therapeutic alliance, which I encounter quite frequently in my work, are explored from the perspective of the therapeutic couple within three-person space, as well as from the perspective of the differing, and sometimes conflicting, functions of mental health care. The implications of this for the provision of psychological therapy for people with severe mental health problems within the NHS are then considered.

[[RunningHead]]

Integration in Psychotherapy: Models and Methods edited by Jeremy Holmes and Anthony Bateman (Oxford University Press, Oxford, 2002). 214 pp. £30.99.

Julian Stern

Julian Stern

Watford Road
Harrow HA1 3UJ

Julian Stern  

This is an ambitious book. Jeremy Holmes and Anthony Bateman are both well respected and prolific writers, both working as Consultant Psychiatrists in Psychotherapy within the NHS. In this multi-author book they attempt to ‘explore and expound … integrative currents as they impact on the spectrum of contemporary psychological therapy’ (p. 2). They continue: ‘(W)e are not advocating integrative therapy as a modality of therapy in its own right, nor do we see it as inherently valuable … Rather we are attempting to capture a particular moment in the history of psychological therapies in which flux, crossover, and recombination … are in the air’ (p. 2).

The book is divided into two parts. In Part I, ‘Theory’, leading practitioners within traditional modes self-critically look outwards towards the limits and links between their own approaches and others. Thus Bateman himself describes ‘Integrative therapy from an analytic perspective’, while Power, Asen and Mace approach the question from the perspectives of Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT), Systemic Therapy and Group Therapy, respectively.

Part II, ‘Models and Practice’, is described as a ‘showcase for the cutting-edge modalities in psychological therapy, all of which are, at this stage of their evolution, integrative’. Chapters in this part are on Cognitive Analytic Therapy (CAT), Psychodynamic Interpersonal Therapy (PIT), Interpersonal Therapy (IPT), Dialectical Behavioural Therapy (DBT), the Therapeutic Community (TC) and Supportive Psychotherapy (LTST — Long Term Supportive Therapy).

The Chapters in Part I, together with that by Denman on CAT, were most interesting to the reader. Bateman gives a historical perspective on integrative therapy from an analytic perspective, starting with Freud's own recommendation of an integration of behavioural and analytic techniques in the treatment of an agoraphobic patient.

Editorial

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence  

In this issue, I am delighted to publish the best papers submitted for the first Essay Prize of the APP Educational Trust.

The Educational Trust took the inspired and very generous decision to award an annual prize of £1000 for the best essay submitted. Their hope was to encourage high standards of scholarship and to support the journal by giving authors an incentive to submit papers to us. In this first year, we received thirteen papers, many of a very high standard. The judging was undertaken by members of the Educational Trust of the APP and members of the Editorial Board of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy.

The winner is Ian Thurston, with his paper Developing the Therapeutic Alliance in Acute Mental Health Care. The runner-up is Hope Samuel, with her paper on Adoption: Some Clinical Features of Adults who have been Adopted and the Difficulties of Helping them in NHS Psychotherapy. Paul Barrows submitted a paper on Change in Parent-Infant Psychotherapy, also judged to be a runner-up, which he has decided to publish elsewhere.

The three Highly Commended papers (in no particular order) are: Joanne Stubley's Bearing the Unbearable: Melancholia following Severe Trauma; John Wright's ‘Things are Changing for me…the Anger is still there but not the Hatred’. Some Reflections from Work with a Racist; and, finally, Looking into Later Life: Psychodynamic Observation and Old Age by Rachael Davenhill, Andrew Balfour, Margaret Rustin, Martin Blanchard and Kate Tress.

Our congratulations to all these authors of excellent papers. Judging the best was indeed a difficult task. I hope that our readers enjoy reading them as much as we did.

A General Practitioner, his Patients and their Feelings: Exploring the Emotions behind Physical Symptoms by Sotiris Zalidis (Free Association Books, London, 2001). 245 pp. £16.95.

Mary Eckert

Mary Eckert

293-299 Kentish Town Road
London NW5 2TJ

Mary Eckert  

This is a book that demonstrates the usefulness of psychodynamic ideas in understanding psychosomatic patients in General Practice. I hope that it will be read by General Practitioners who are not so familiar with such an approach. It should also be of interest to psychotherapists and other health workers who encounter difficulties in treating people for whom psychosomatic symptoms cause problems in functioning emotionally.

The book shows how an extraordinary man, Dr Sotiris Zalidis, can listen ‘with his heart’ and convert the understanding of somatic symptoms into meaningful communications about what is going on within the patient emotionally; which can lead to relief of physical symptoms and associated anxieties and enable the patient to recognize and feel underlying emotions. This in turn can improve the quality of the patient's relationship to himself and others.

responseSet.title: Religious Fundamentalism and Primitive Projective Processes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Religious Fundamentalism and Primitive Projective Processes

James Springett

James Springett

32 Leek Road, Buxton
Derbyshire SK17 6UE

James Springett  

In her book on the origins, nature and contemporary global significance of religious fundamentalism, Karen Armstrong cites the example of an early twentieth-century, ultra-Orthodox Jewish spirituality1 vehemently opposed to the creation of the state of Israel. Armstrong suggests that

This rejectionist vision is utterly incomprehensible to Jews who regard the Zionist achievement as wondrous and salvific. This is the dilemma that Jews, Christians and Muslims have all had to face in the twentieth century: between the fundamentalists and those who adopt a more positive attitude to the modern world there is an impassable gulf. Rational arguments are of no avail, because the divergence springs from a deeper and more instinctual level of the mind (Armstrong 2000, pp. 204-205 [my highlighting]).

This paper will try and show that Melanie Klein's depiction of primitive mental processes serves to elucidate both the nature of this ‘impassable gulf’ and the ‘deeper’ levels of psychic functioning from which it originates. By shedding a specifically Kleinian psychoanalytic and object relations light on what appears to both warrant and inform the discourse on fundamentalism, it hopes to show how individual and group formation, and the interaction between them, are profoundly influenced by unconscious processes. Such processes are shown to be characterized by mechanisms of defence against anxieties — mechanisms induced by changes that threaten existing social relationships (Jaques 1955, p. 479).

Evidence in the Psychological Therapies: A Critical Guide for Practitioners edited by C. Mace, S. Moorey and B. Roberts (Brunner-Routledge, Hove and Philadelphia, 2001). 218 pp. £15.99.

Maria Eyres

Maria Eyres

Bow Road, London E3 4LL

Maria Eyres  

In preparation for the Part II of the examination for the Membership of the Royal College of Psychiatrists (MRCPsych), I had to familiarize myself with the tenets of evidence-based medicine (EBM) as it now comprises a substantial part of the examination. Most publications and courses I came across seemed to focus on biological treatments; I was therefore delighted to come across this book, hoping that it would fill a substantial gap.

The book has its roots in a conference organized by the Psychotherapy Faculty of the Royal College of Psychiatrists. In the first chapter, two of the editors, Chris Mace and Stirling Moorey, set the tone by stating that the book ‘should ensure that a psychotherapist, whatever his or her interests, is not only better informed about the clinical implications of evidence-based practice but better able to recognize its strengths and weaknesses, and able to meet its requirements at the level of service organization’ (p. 1).

Following the introductory chapter, the book can be seen to be loosely divided into two parts; the first one focusing on the fact that evidence is a very complex concept in itself and it becomes even more so in the field of psychological treatments. The authors examine the meaning of evidence at length, looking at it from various points of view, including the law where John Jackson explores the nature of evidence in the judicial system and concludes that it is indeed very different from the scientific model with which we are familiar. This part of the book is very thought-provoking, challenging and polemical at times, inviting the reader to form their own views on the subject rather than spoon feeding them ready-made answers. It becomes clear that evidence in psychotherapy is diverse and can not be seen as homogenous; while powerful at its best, it also has serious limitations. In addition, the very nature of the psychological treatments creates major difficulties in conducting the gold standard of EBM, a randomized controlled trial (RCT). Despite these obstacles, Simon Wessely argues that RCTs are rightly placed at the top of the hierarchy of evidence. Among other contributors here, Paul Sturdee in particular questions the objectivity of evidence, and draws a list of ‘likely winners and losers’ from EBM. This is one of the strengths of this publication; the authors critically appraise the nature, sources and legitimacy of evidence itself before assessing the quality of research and discussing how it could be used in practice.

Self-Harm: A Psychotherapeutic Approach by Fiona Gardner (Brunner-Routledge, Hove and Philadelphia, 2001). 163 pp. £14.95.

Rosemary Loshak

Rosemary Loshak

62 Roman Road, London E2 0PG

Rosemary Loshak  

Those working with adolescents and young women in mental health settings, in children's homes, in the NHS, in social services or in the voluntary sector, will find this book an invaluable and thought provoking resource. Gardner presents clinical material which explores and throws light on the internal worlds of a small group of young women seen or assessed for individual psychotherapy during her work in a counselling setting and in private practice, yet it is her own much broader and long experience of social work with very marginalized and damaged young people which forms a solid foundation to her therapeutic knowledge.

Reading Gardner's account of cutting, the particular self-destructive behaviour which provides a focus for the book, I was reminded of a young woman who presented herself immaculately made-up and smart in T-shirt and jeans; the jeans were decorated with a fine tracing of silver lines crisscrossing the denim fabric to create a series of delicately drawn spider's webs. These silver lines seemed to me to be a visual reminder of the razor cuts drawn across her arms and legs, which lay hidden beneath her mask and which she could not yet speak about directly. The careful cover-up seemed to reflect the multiple layers of meaning underlying such disturbing behaviour which Gardner elucidates so clearly.

The cutting is paradoxical; devastatingly self-destructive, it is also experienced by the young women as creative, as a release of unbearable tension, and as a means of continuing to live. It is also metaphor, expressing a conflict which cannot be communicated in any other way, and in so doing, offering ‘a route into contact with the world of words’ and hope of a new beginning. The conflict is firstly one of adolescence, for the behaviour typically occurs among adolescent girls or in young women who remain stuck in an adolescent state of mind. The developmental task of adolescence is that of individuation and separation from dependent ties to the parents. This occurs both concretely in relation to the actual parents and unconsciously in relation to the internalized parental figures of early childhood. The cutting is, therefore, also expressive of a deeper conflict at the unconscious level of early object relations.

Adoption: Some Clinical Features of Adults Who Have Been Adopted and the Difficulties of Helping Them in Nhs Psychotherapy

Hope Samuel

Hope Samuel

5 Briardale Gardens
London NW3 7PN

Hope Samuel  

This paper explores issues of adoption and specifically what happens when adults who have been adopted as babies present for psychological help in the NHS. Two clinical examples of women patients are given, and it is suggested that there is a re-enactment of earlier adoption experiences within the approach to and engagement in psychotherapy. The tendency for splitting and projection between biological and adoptive mothers, good and bad mothers and the patient's body and mind are discussed, as are the implications for the transference and countertransference. The combination of the patients’ problems and the restrictions imposed on treatment in the NHS due to scarce resources is explored.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Therapy in Primary Care: Work in Progress

Marina Perris

Marina Perris

34 Talbot Road, London N6 4QP
e-mail: m.perris@lineone.net

Marina Perris  

This paper considers the benefits to the mental health of patients of the provision of psychological services in primary care. I describe by means of context, the development of such a service over the last 7 years. In the following section I discuss the question of who is ‘the patient’ who gets referred and who can benefit from psychological treatment in a primary care setting. More specifically I consider the spectrum of psychopathology of patients who are referred in primary care for psychological help and in relation to this question I discuss what would constitute a meaningful clinical intervention. In the last section, I look at the question of what needs to be in place for a successful psychological service in a primary care setting.

I argue that, while benefits for mild to moderate psychological problems have been documented by research findings, clinical scrutiny of the interventions in primary care suggest that this is not necessarily the case when it comes to patients with more severe psychological problems. I further argue that there are still important issues to be considered and researched and structures to be put into place before we can safeguard the clinical effectiveness of psychological interventions in primary care.

Psychoanalytic Theory: An Introduction by Anthony Elliott, second edition (Palgrave, Basingstoke, 2002). 196 pp. £18.99.

Priscilla Kent

Priscilla Kent

London E1 1BB

Priscilla Kent  

This book, by the Director of the Centre for Critical Theory at the University of West of England, sets out to argue that psychoanalytic theory is an inherently critical, political discourse. Elliott suggests that psychoanalysis involves the mixing of repressed desire and power relations, unconscious passion and cultural reproduction. Although he says that the cultural and institutional processes of modernization, including global expansion have ‘reached into the heart of selfhood and created new forms of personal identity’, his argument for extending the theory of psychoanalysis (of the self) to cultural and social issues relies on quoting statements from cultural critics such as Christopher Lasch and other critical theorists, to the effect that in our contemporary cultural condition the self has narcissistically turned back upon itself. Elliott posits that the waning of political life in the modern world has led to a shrinkage of the self. However, I would have liked to see a fuller argument vindicating the application of psychoanalytic theory to explain current social experiences.

In his introduction, Elliott explains that he has expanded on his first edition by incorporating detailed biographical accounts of some of the main psychoanalytic theorists, that is Freud, Klein, Winnicott and Lacan. Indeed, he provides some interesting background information which, as a psychiatrist myself, I found insightful when thinking on their particular theories. Also included in the second edition is new material on the psychodynamics of race and post-colonialism, as well as a newly reorganized chapter, ‘Psychoanalytic Feminism’. I found the added section on the writings of queer theorist Judith Butler both concise and comprehensive. A further new addition is the lucid summary of the works of the neo-Lacanian, Slavoj Zizek. Elliott obviously admires this Slovene theorist, and his enthusiasm shows itself to the reader in a lively manner.

I was somewhat surprised at the scope of the book, and a little overwhelmed at the number of theorists cited.

The Resistances to Psycho-Analysis: An Old Devil in Modern Clothing?

Jessica Burnett-Stuart

Jessica Burnett-Stuart

114 Southmoor Road
Oxford OX2 6RB
e-mail: jess@almagest.fsworld.co.uk

Jessica Burnett-Stuart  

This paper addresses the organizational resistance to psycho-analytic thinking within the public services. The paper begins with a look at Freud's proposal that resistance to psycho-analytic theory is provoked by his assertion that sexual impulses underlie much of human activity. The author suggests that similar unconscious resistance to psychoanalytic thinking is present in organizations today, but that which we in the modern world find more unacceptable than sex as such is to be confronted with our own essential vulnerability. It is argued that because psychoanalytic therapy makes us aware of the limits of our ability to control our emotional experience, it evokes resistance at the individual and organizational level. Therapies that promote mastery and control are more acceptable because they allow these defences to be maintained and sometimes reinforced. The consequences of this in terms of loss of creativity are explored.

Looking into Later Life: Psychodynamic Observation and Old Age

Rachael Davenhill , Andrew Balfour , Margaret Rustin , Martin Blanchard and Kate Tress

Kate Tress

120 Belsize Lane
London NW3 5BA

Pond Street
London NW3 2QG

Kate Tress  

Psychodynamic observation has been used successfully as a core component of training for child and adult psychotherapists within the NHS. This paper will describe the use of the psychodynamic observational method in the multidisciplinary training of health professionals working with older adults.

In taking on the role of receptive observer within the framework provided by the psychodynamic observational method, it is hoped that the observer will come closer to the older person's experience and develop an attuned capacity to see and retain detail. In becoming aware of the emotional impact the interplay between the individual and their environment may produce, participants will learn from their own experience about factors, conscious and unconscious, which can support or impede development and adjustment to transitions in the later part of life. It allows thinking in depth to take place about the experience of the older person as well as the difficulties encountered in the caring role.

The first half of the paper will describe the ‘how to’ of setting up a psychodynamic observation. The second half will describe two observations, one in a more normative setting of an outpatient health clinic for older people, and the other in a nursing home where the older person and staff are confronted with a greater degree of physical and mental deterioration.

[[RunningHead]]

The Importance of Fathers. A Psychoanalytic Re-evaluation edited by Judith Trowell and Alicia Etchegoyen (The New Library of Psychoanalysis, 42. Brunner-Routledge, London, 2002). vii + 255 pp. £17.99.

Wilhelm Skogstad

Wilhelm Skogstad

Richmond
Surrey TW10 7JF

Wilhelm Skogstad  

This is an important book. Psychoanalysis has, in its history, not been very balanced in its views of fathers. While Freud saw the father as ‘all-important’ (as Britton puts it in his chapter), the pendulum later swung the other way and for a long time many psychoanalytic theories saw the mother as ‘all-important’. In recent times there has been much more of an acknowledgement of the important role of the father: in the USA, particularly on the basis of the infant research by Mahler, Abelin and others; in Britain, for example in the post-Kleinian development on the Oedipus complex; and in France, specifically through Lacan's contributions. By bringing together many of the existing ideas and putting forward new ones, this book goes a long way in redressing the balance.

It is also a rich and diverse book that looks at many aspects and from different perspectives. While all the authors are psychoanalysts or psychoanalytic psychotherapists from Britain and so their contributions are all rooted in British object relations theory, they cover the range of views from the three groups of British psychoanalysis as well as important aspects from American and French psychoanalysis. The contributions in the book are based on a whole variety of sources: from developmental and clinical research, from individual psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy with adults, adolescents and children, from infant observation, perinatal interventions and family work. The different chapters look at very different aspects: the role of father in child development and in the internal world; the changes in becoming and being a father; the difficulty for some mothers in allowing the father a place with the child, and the characteristics of women who overvalue their fathers to the detriment of their femininity; the effects of absence, death or disability of the father; the role of father in the boy's gender identity; and other aspects, such as adoptive fathers and grandfathers. On the whole, even though differing in its emphasis between chapters, the book offers a differentiated balance between the role of father in the internal and the external world and the link between the two.

responseSet.title: Triangulation or Strangulation: Managing the Suicidal Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Triangulation or Strangulation: Managing the Suicidal Patient

Narendra Keval

Narendra Keval

The Avenue, Watford, Herts, WD17 3NU.

Narendra Keval  

The suicidal person is tortured by an internal dilemma to do with unbearable feelings of separation and loss which seem to be insoluble. The suicidal act is an attempt to resolve this by creating a phantasy in which the intolerable pain associated with a grievance is projected and obliterated by murdering the body that has become identified with it. This type of assault on the self reveals a major confusion between mind and body, affecting the capacity to think and resulting in two-dimensional, rather than three-dimensional, thinking, which can permeate therapeutic efforts, at both individual and systemic levels, in the way these patients are managed by their carers. It is suggested that a safety-net made up of mindful carers, based on a triangular principle, can provide a symbolic or mental space, and a containment for the patient's intolerable states of mind, which can be analogous to an ego-function that is lacking internally, and works well if the net can manage to function as a healthy parental couple. Two case studies are presented as an attempt to illuminate some of the dynamics and difficulties in maintaining the safety-net.

responseSet.title: Working with Bangladeshi Young Women
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working with Bangladeshi Young Women

Rosemary Loshak

Rosemary Loshak

62 Roman Road, London E2 0PG

Rosemary Loshak  

The author describes once-weekly psychoanalytically informed work in a child mental health setting with young Bangladeshi women making the transition to adulthood. She argues that the prevalent notion of ‘culture conflict’ used to account for patterns of emotional disturbance and self-harm in these young women is reductionist and represents a denial of severe family dysfunction and individual psychic pain. She shows how, despite cultural differences, psychoanalytically informed thinking can provide a basis for understanding these young women's experience and facilitate their emotional growth. However, faced with complex unconscious processes, the worker has to remain alert to her countertransference in order not to be drawn into an unhelpful collusion or denial of reality. Organizational defences may also operate to protect staff and their institutions from awareness of such high levels of emotional distress, which therefore remain hidden. The author is grateful to the two young women who consented to publication in the hope that it might help others. Details have been changed to protect their anonymity.

responseSet.title: Menstruation — the Gap in the Text?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Menstruation — the Gap in the Text?

Patricia Kerkham

Patricia Kerkham

Psychoanalytic Psychotherapist
Banklands, Clenchwarton
Kings Lynn, Norfolk PE34 4DB

Patricia Kerkham  

Recent advances in neuroscience provide compelling evidence of the significance of our embodiment for our psychic life, and the extent to which subjectivity is both a social and a psychological phenomenon. In thinking about the experiential sense of living in an explicitly female body, I suggest that the connotations of disgust with which menstruation is associated cannot fail to affect women's core sense of identity and subjectivity. As gendered blood, to no small degree separating and defining women, the way that it is consciously and unconsciously represented contributes to the way that difference is experienced by every female. We cannot fail, in part, to become what we are attributed to be.

In this paper I have tried to think about the ways in which psychoanalysis appears to mirror a cultural avoidance, which in part it helps to promote, whereby menstruation represents the concealed nature of female sexuality, in contrast to which the phallus continually signifies itself. When acknowledged at all, menstruation is seen as a loss, an incontinence, or an absence (of babies) — a symbol of waste and a break in productivity. I have considered ways in which, and reasons why, images of blood and women can be seen as a sign pointing to a reality against which other variables are measured. Within this paradigm I have attempted to consider ways in which menstruation can be considered to be a metaphor for women's symbolic and real absence, marginalization, fragile status, inarticulacy and misrepresentation.

The Psychodynamics of Addiction edited by Martin Weegmann and Robert Cohen (Whurr Publishers, London, 2002). 180 pp. £19.50.

Alistair Sweet

Alistair Sweet

40 Elmwood Avenue
Belfast BT9 6AZ

Alistair Sweet  

The Psychodynamics of Addiction, edited by Martin Weegmann and Robert Cohen, is one of those rare publications that one can quite confidently predict will become a classic in its genre. On one level, this observation perhaps reflects the paucity of psychodynamic literature devoted to furthering our understanding of addictive processes; however, more pointedly, credit is due to Weegmann and Cohen for their judicious and insightful presentation of a fascinating series of papers by clinicians working in this challenging field.

The book is presented in four distinct parts, with sections on: theoretical approaches to addiction, therapy, supervision and countertransference, and a closing chapter on family dynamics and addiction. One of the most pleasing aspects of the volume is the adroit application of the theoretical perspectives, cogently presented in Part 1, to the highly informative and interesting clinical vignettes presented in the later chapters. In addition, I found the willingness to explore the diversity within psychodynamic understanding, as this applies to addiction, really refreshing.

Part 1 of the collection is entitled A Review of Different Schools, with the editors, very sensibly in my view, electing to present a detailed consideration of three key theorists and the application of their views to addictive processes. Wilfred Bion's model of the container and the contained is explored in Chapter 1, followed by a chapter on attachment, and finally one on Kohut's investigation of narcissistic vulnerability. I found the opening chapter on containment, by Arthur Hyatt Williams, highly informative and quite gripping. Hyatt Williams explores the connection between infantile feelings of emptiness or the distress of separation and the later attempt to contain these, otherwise uncontainable feelings, through recourse to drugs. While the chapter acknowledges the early analytic contributions on addiction by authors such as Simmel, Rado, and Glover in the 1930s, it is the application of a Kleinian perspective that is most persuasive here. Rosenfeld's paper on drug addiction from 1960 is reconsidered, particularly in relation to transference dynamics with addicted patients.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bearing the Unbearable: Melancholia following Severe Trauma

Joanne Stubley

Joanne Stubley

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA
e-mail: jostubley@hotmail.com

Joanne Stubley  

In presenting an account of a psychoanalytically informed therapy with a severely traumatized patient, this paper attempts to address questions of how such a patient can psychically survive such a trauma, and how she may make use of her objects. What kind of countertransference experience the therapist may need to bear and what may be the aim of such kinds of therapy is considered.

[[RunningHead]]

Observation Observed: Closely Observed Infants on Film. A Tavistock Foundation film using observational material from the BBC (copyright). Written by Margaret Rustin and Beth Miller. Accompanying booklet by Lisa Miller. Copies from the Tavistock Clinic, £50 or £25 for students.

Julia Fabricius

Julia Fabricius

39 Willes Road
London NW5 3DN

Julia Fabricius  

The two videos in this set, each about an hour long, were produced by the Tavistock Foundation out of film material made during the filming for the BBC series, Talking Cure, on the work of the Tavistock Clinic. The material for the video is taken from two observations undertaken by students of the Tavistock Clinic, one a baby observation and the other a young child observation which starts when the child is 2 years old. Both observations were set up specially, with the family giving permission not only for an observer but also for a BBC camera and operator. The film material and the editing of the film are, as one would expect from its professional production, of very high quality.

The first video shows something of the process of infant observation and highlights a number of issues. It is in the style of a documentary. This enables the viewer to really see something of the development of these children in the context of their families and also to get a little of the feel of the experience of doing an infant or young child observation. The cutting between different levels of situation — observations of the children, film of the observers observing, comments the observers make about their experience when outside the situation, comments of the mother about the experience, film of the seminar discussing the observations, comments by teachers at the Tavistock Clinic and commentary by the BBC commentator — is well done. Points are highlighted and things are explained and watching the video gives an integrated experience.

The first video is in four sections. The first starts with the birth of baby Damian and then shows the setting up of both the observations. The other sections ‘Identifications’, ‘Observing and being observed’ and ‘Containment and endings’ focus on a range of issues that can be broadly grouped under those themes. For example, the way in which the observer can identify with the child or with the parent is explicated and the question of feeling excluded and what this can teach about Oedipal issues is highlighted. These themes are explored in a way that is engaging and moving.
